luis-de-onis.name: Luis de Onís y González-Vara
luis-de-onis.narrative: Spanish career diplomat. Educated at the University of Salamanca by age 16. Joined his uncle's legation in Dresden in 1780. Negotiated the Adams–Onís Treaty (1819) with U.S. Secretary of State John Quincy Adams, ceding Florida to the United States. Awarded the hereditary title Regidor Perpetuo de Salamanca (1818). Published a two-volume memoir of the negotiations (1820).
keyFacts: Educated at University of Salamanca by age 16
Joined uncle's legation in Dresden (1780)
Negotiated Adams–Onís Treaty ceding Florida to the U.S. (1819)
Awarded Regidor Perpetuo de Salamanca (1818)
Published two-volume memoir of negotiations (1820)

federica-von-mercklein.name: Federica Cristiana Juana von Mercklein Schrimer
federica-von-mercklein.narrative: German noblewoman, presumably from the Saxon court milieu where Luis was stationed. Married Luis in Dresden in 1788 when he was 26 and she was 21. Their marriage represents the family's first cross-cultural union — a Spanish diplomat and a German noblewoman. She died in 1817 at age 50, two years before the Adams–Onís Treaty that would make her husband famous.

narcisa-de-onis.name: Narcisa de Onís y Mercklein
narcisa-de-onis.narrative: Second child of Luis de Onís and Federica von Mercklein.

clementina-de-onis.name: Clementina/Joaquina de Onís y Mercklein
clementina-de-onis.narrative: Third child of Luis de Onís and Federica von Mercklein.

mauricio-carlos.name: Mauricio Carlos de Onís y Mercklein
mauricio-carlos.role: Minister of State (1840); President of Congress of Deputies
mauricio-carlos.narrative: Eldest son of Luis. Born in Dresden during his father's diplomatic posting. Became a Spanish politician and diplomat in his own right, serving as Minister of State in 1840 and as President of the Congress of Deputies. Married his first cousin Carolina de Onís in Cantalapiedra in 1816 — a match that produced the distinctive double surname 'Onís y Onís' in their children.
keyFacts: Born in Dresden during father's diplomatic posting
Minister of State of Spain (1840)
President of the Congress of Deputies
Married first cousin Carolina de Onís (1816)
Died in ancestral town of Cantalapiedra

carolina-de-onis.name: Carolina María Antonia de Onís y Navarro-Pinillos
carolina-de-onis.role: Wife of Mauricio Carlos; first cousin
carolina-de-onis.narrative: Mauricio Carlos's first cousin — herself from the Onís family. This first-cousin marriage produced the distinctive double surname 'Onís y Onís' that persisted in family memory. The marriage took place in Cantalapiedra, the ancestral town.

luis-carlos.name: Luis Carlos de Onís y Onís
luis-carlos.narrative: Third child of Mauricio Carlos and Carolina. Identified in PARES as the father of José María Onís López. His wife's surname was apparently López, given his son's full name. Further biographical details remain to be established through Salamanca or Cantalapiedra records.
keyFacts: Son of Mauricio Carlos and Carolina (first-cousin marriage)
Bore the distinctive double surname 'Onís y Onís'
Father of José María, future University librarian
Wife's surname was López (identity unknown)

unknown-lopez.name: [Unknown — surname López]
unknown-lopez.narrative: Identity unknown. Her surname was apparently López, based on her son José María's patronymic (Onís López). Cantalapiedra or Salamanca parish records should identify her.

jose-maria.name: José María Onís López
jose-maria.role: Head Librarian & Archivist, University of Salamanca
jose-maria.narrative: Studied Law and Philosophy at the Universidad Central de Madrid. Head Librarian and Archivist of the University of Salamanca for his entire career. Close personal friend of Miguel de Unamuno, the philosopher and Rector. Described in the PARES record as the 'patriarca de la familia Onís.' His friendship with Unamuno created the intellectual world in which his son Federico would grow up.
keyFacts: Studied Law and Philosophy, Universidad Central de Madrid
Head Librarian and Archivist, University of Salamanca
Close friend of Miguel de Unamuno
Described as 'patriarca de la familia Onís'
Great-grandson of diplomat Luis de Onís

unknown-sanchez.name: [Unknown — surname Sánchez]
unknown-sanchez.narrative: Identity unknown. Her surname was apparently Sánchez, based on her son Federico's full name (Onís Sánchez). Salamanca civil records should confirm.

federico.name: Federico de Onís Sánchez
federico.role: Founded Columbia's Dept. of Spanish, Hispanic Institute, RHM
federico.narrative: Studied under Unamuno at Salamanca and Menéndez Pidal in Madrid. Married Andrea Rodríguez Bondía in Salamanca in 1908, establishing a household at the Newburgh property (1924). His marriage to Andrea produced two sons: José María and Arturo. In 1916, he was invited to Columbia by President Nicholas Murray Butler. On August 20, 1924, he married Harriet Wishnieff. Founded the Department of Spanish, the Instituto de las Españas (1920), the Revista Hispánica Moderna (1934), and the Department of Hispanic Studies at the University of Puerto Rico (1927). His scholarly network was extraordinary, including García Lorca, Antonio Machado, and Juan Ramón Jiménez. Published Gabriela Mistral's first book (1922), helping launch her Nobel Prize–winning career. Never returned to Spain after the Civil War. A street in Salamanca bears his name.
keyFacts: Studied under Unamuno at Salamanca
Married Andrea Rodríguez Bondía (1908); sons José María, Federico Jr., and Arturo
Established Newburgh property (1924)
Married Harriet Wishnieff (August 20, 1924)
Invited to Columbia University (1916)
Founded Instituto de las Españas (1920)
Published Gabriela Mistral's first book (1922)
Founded Revista Hispánica Moderna (1934)
Network included García Lorca, Machado, Juan Ramón Jiménez
A street in Salamanca bears his name

andrea-rodriguez.name: Andrea Rodríguez Bondía
andrea-rodriguez.role: First wife of Federico de Onís
andrea-rodriguez.narrative: Married Federico in Salamanca in 1908, when he was 22. They had three sons together: José María, Federico Jr., and Arturo. After Federico left for Columbia University in 1916, Andrea remained in Spain with her three young sons. She provided crucial stability during a period of political and personal upheaval. Despite the separation, she maintained the family's connection to Salamanca and its scholarly traditions.

harriet-wishnieff.name: Harriet Wishnieff de Onís
harriet-wishnieff.role: Translator; established canon of Latin American literature in English
harriet-wishnieff.narrative: Arguably as consequential as her husband in shaping the relationship between Latin American and North American culture. Born in small-town Illinois, she moved to New York to study foreign languages at Barnard College (B.A. 1916). Between 1930 and 1969, she published approximately 40 book translations from Spanish and Portuguese into English — making her the first truly prolific translator of Latin American literature into English. Chilean writer José Donoso said she 'controlled the sluices of the circulation of Latin American literature in the United States.' In 1952, she recommended Borges to Knopf; the editor declined. She died in 1969 finishing her translation of Jorge Amado's Dona Flor and Her Two Husbands. Confirmed as Juan's mother per Vitrina 2. Federico called her 'Jarrie,' and she called him 'Federrico.'
keyFacts: B.A. Barnard College (1916)
Worked as secretary for Isadora Duncan
~40 book translations from Spanish/Portuguese to English
Recommended Borges to Knopf in 1952 (declined)
Translated Ortiz's Cuban Counterpoint (1947)
Translated Carpentier's The Lost Steps (1956)
Confirmed as Juan's mother (Vitrina 2)

federico-jr.name: Federico de Onís (hijo)
federico-jr.narrative: Eldest child of Federico de Onís and Andrea Rodríguez Bondía, born in 1909 (per Ancestry records). Remained in Spain with his mother and brothers after Federico's departure for Columbia University in 1916. Died in 1967, just one year after his father. Further biographical details pending confirmation.
keyFacts: Son of Federico de Onís and Andrea Rodríguez Bondía
Born 1909, died 1967 (per Ancestry records)
Remained in Spain with mother
Further details pending confirmation

juan-journalist.name: Juan de Onís
juan-journalist.role: Foreign correspondent, NYT & LA Times (26 years)
juan-journalist.narrative: Foreign correspondent and bureau chief for the New York Times and Los Angeles Times for 26 years. Covered Latin America (especially Brazil), the Middle East, Africa, Washington, and the United Nations. Author of The Green Cathedral: Sustainable Development of Amazonia (Oxford, 1992) and co-author of The Alliance That Lost Its Way (1979). Translator of José Martí's Nuestra América. Confirmed as son of Harriet Wishnieff.
keyFacts: Foreign correspondent, NYT and LA Times (26 years)
Covered Latin America, Middle East, Africa, Washington, UN
Author: The Green Cathedral (Oxford, 1992)
Co-author: The Alliance That Lost Its Way (1979)
Translator of José Martí's Nuestra América
Son of Federico de Onís and Harriet Wishnieff

paco.name: Paco de Onís
paco.role: Exec. Dir., Skylight; human rights documentary filmmaker
paco.narrative: Executive Director and Executive Producer of Skylight, a Brooklyn-based human rights media organization co-founded with Pamela Yates and Peter Kinoy. His given name is Juan de Onís, but he goes by Paco. Grew up in Argentina and Brazil during military dictatorships. Multilingual (Spanish, Portuguese, English, Italian, French). First married Jody Hagberg, with whom he had his son Alejandro. Later married Pamela Yates. Attended the Salamanca exhibition with Pamela. Producing credits include Granito: How to Nail a Dictator (Sundance 2011), 500 Years (Sundance 2017), State of Fear, and Borderland (2024). Previously produced for PBS (with Bill Moyers) and National Geographic.
keyFacts: Co-founded Skylight (1981) with Pamela Yates & Peter Kinoy
Given name Juan de Onís; goes by Paco
Grew up in Argentina and Brazil during dictatorships
Multilingual: Spanish, Portuguese, English, Italian, French
First wife: Jody Hagberg (mother of Alejandro)
Second wife: Pamela Yates
Attended Salamanca exhibition with Pamela Yates
Witness to War — Academy Award (1985)
Granito — Sundance premiere (2011)
500 Years — Sundance premiere (2017)

pamela-yates.name: Pamela Yates
pamela-yates.narrative: Paco's second wife and creative/professional partner at Skylight for over three decades. Left her hometown at 16 for New York City. Guggenheim Fellow. Sundance Special Jury Award (When the Mountains Tremble, 1984), Academy Award as Executive Producer (Witness to War, 1985), Emmy Award, Overseas Press Club Award (State of Fear, 2006). Her film Granito was used as forensic evidence in the genocide trial of Guatemalan dictator Efraín Ríos Montt. Step-mother to Alejandro de Onís.
keyFacts: B.A. University of Massachusetts, Amherst (1976)
Guggenheim Fellow
Academy Award: Witness to War (1985)
Emmy Award
Granito used as evidence in Ríos Montt genocide trial
Poynter Fellowship in Journalism, Yale (2024)

jody-hagberg.name: Jody Hagberg
jody-hagberg.role: First wife of Paco de Onís; mother of Alejandro
jody-hagberg.narrative: Paco's first wife and mother of Alejandro de Onís. Further details pending.

alejandro.name: Alejandro de Onís
alejandro.narrative: VP of Communications at the Knight Foundation. Son of Paco de Onís and Jody Hagberg. Carries forward the family's multi-generational engagement with the Hispanic world, public discourse, and storytelling through institutional communications, strategic philanthropy, and the intersection of technology and media.

camila.name: Camila de Onís
camila.narrative: Daughter of Paco de Onís. Member of the eighth generation of the de Onís family.

jose-maria-onis-bondia.name: José de Onís
jose-maria-onis-bondia.role: Professor, University of Colorado Boulder; author and scholar of Hispanic-American studies
jose-maria-onis-bondia.narrative: José de Onís was born in Oviedo, Spain, the son of Federico de Onís and Andrea Rodríguez Bondía. He became a prolific scholar and professor in the Department of Spanish and Portuguese at the University of Colorado Boulder, where a graduate achievement award still bears his name. His major works include The United States as Seen by Spanish American Writers, 1776–1890 (Hispanic Institute, 1952; 26 editions, held by 539 libraries worldwide), The Hispanic Contribution to the State of Colorado (Westview Press, 1976; 14 chapters by leading scholars), Las Misiones Españolas en los Estados Unidos (University of Colorado, 1958–59), and Melville y el Mundo Hispánico: Nueve Conferencias y un Prólogo (1974). His earlier academic work La Lengua Popular Madrileña en la Obra de Pérez Galdós (1949) demonstrated his linguistic scholarship. He served in military intelligence during World War II, an experience later captured in The Eagle and the Sparrow: The Second World War Memoirs of José de Onís, as told to his grandson Louis Gray Kunkel. José was deeply invested in preserving the de Onís family history and wrote extensively about the family's connections to the Hispanic world across centuries.
keyFacts: Born January 28, 1911 in Oviedo, Asturias, Spain
Died September 10, 2003 in Bozeman, Montana
Son of Federico de Onís and Andrea Rodríguez Bondía
Professor, Dept. of Spanish and Portuguese, University of Colorado Boulder
Jose De Onis Achievement Award — scholarship at CU Boulder named in his honor
The United States as Seen by Spanish American Writers, 1776–1890 (1952) — 26 editions, 539 libraries
The Hispanic Contribution to the State of Colorado (Westview Press, 1976)
Las Misiones Españolas en los Estados Unidos (1958–59)
Melville y el Mundo Hispánico (1974) — 9 lectures and a prologue
La Lengua Popular Madrileña en la Obra de Pérez Galdós (1949)
Los Estados Unidos Vistos por Escritores Hispanoamericanos (1956) — Spanish edition, 134 libraries
The Eagle and the Sparrow — WWII memoirs, as told to grandson Louis Gray Kunkel
Served in military intelligence during World War II

arturo-de-onis.name: Arturo de Onís
arturo-de-onis.role: Son of Federico; remained in Spain with his mother
arturo-de-onis.narrative: Youngest son of Federico de Onís and Andrea Rodríguez Bondía. Remained in Spain with his mother when Federico decided to stay permanently in New York. Mentioned in a letter from María de Unamuno, who noted that Arturo also wanted to go to the United States.
keyFacts: Son of Federico and Andrea
Remained in Spain with his mother Andrea
Mentioned in letter from María de Unamuno
